title: Working with parts that hold body-based memories
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
Your body keeps the score before your mind can speak 

 I was sitting on my couch, completely still, when my hands started shaking. Not the kind of shake...
htmlContent:
Your body keeps the score before your mind can speak

I was sitting on my couch, completely still, when my hands started shaking. Not the kind of shake you get from too much coffee. This was different. Deep. Involuntary. My palms were sweating and my chest felt tight, and I hadn't thought about anything upsetting. I wasn't remembering anything traumatic. I was just sitting there, watching TV, and my body decided it was time to relive something I didn't even know was still in there.

Here's the thing. Your body holds memories your mind can't access. Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," spent decades showing us that trauma isn't just a story we tell about what happened. It's a physical imprint. A cellular echo. Your nervous system remembers the smell, the temperature of the room, the pressure of a hand on your shoulder, even when your conscious brain has filed the event away as "over and done with."

But here's what I didn't understand for a long time. Those body-based memories don't belong to "you" in the way you think they do. They belong to parts of you. Young parts. Frozen parts. Parts that got stuck in time because the experience was too much for your system to process in the moment.

I'm going to show you how to work with those parts. Not by forcing them to talk. Not by trying to "release" the trauma through some aggressive technique. But by sitting with them, noticing what they're carrying, and letting them know they're not alone anymore. Stay with me here.

Why parts hold body memories differently than the mind does

Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems, talks about parts as distinct subpersonalities that develop to protect us. Some parts are young. Some are angry. Some carry shame so heavy it feels like concrete in your chest. And some parts hold body-based memories that have no words attached to them.

Think about it. When you were a kid and something overwhelming happened, you didn't have the language to process it. Your prefrontal cortex wasn't fully developed. Your brain's hippocampus, which helps contextualize memories, was still forming. So what did your system do? It stored the experience in your body. In your muscles. In your fascia. In the way you hold your shoulders or clench your jaw.

I remember working with a part that showed up as a knot in my stomach. Every time I tried to relax, the knot tightened. Every time I tried to breathe into it, it got harder. I thought I was doing something wrong. I thought I was broken. But what I eventually learned was that the knot was a young part. A part that had been holding a memory of being told I was too much, too sensitive, too emotional. That part couldn't speak. It couldn't form words. All it could do was clench.

Janina Fisher, who wrote extensively about trauma and the body, calls this "procedural memory." It's memory stored in the body's procedures, its habits, its automatic responses. You don't remember the event as a story. You remember it as a sensation. A posture. A flinch. A freeze.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helps you understand why those parts formed in the first place, why your body learned to hold tension as a survival strategy. Because here's the hard truth: your parts didn't develop these body memories because something was wrong with you. They developed them because something was wrong with your environment, and your system did the best it could to keep you safe.

How to approach parts that hold body memories without retraumatizing yourself

This is where most people get it wrong. They try to "process" body memories by diving straight into the sensation. They think if they just feel it fully, it will release. And sometimes that works. But often, it retraumatizes the part that's holding the memory. It floods the system. It makes the part feel like the danger is happening right now, all over again.

So here's what I do now, and what I teach others to do. Start with the part that's watching the part. The part of you that notices the knot in your stomach. That's not the same as the knot itself. That's your observing self, your "Self" in IFS terms. That part is calm. Curious. Compassionate. And it's already separate from the body memory.

From that observing place, you can gently approach the part that's holding the memory. Not to fix it. Not to make it go away. Just to say hello. To notice where it lives in your body. To feel its texture, its temperature, its weight. Does it feel hot or cold? Does it have a shape? Does it move or stay still?

I did this recently with a part that showed up as pressure in my chest. I didn't try to breathe into it. I didn't try to relax it. I just noticed it. I said to it, internally, "I see you there. I know you're holding something. You don't have to tell me about it right now. I'm just here with you."

Wild, right? The pressure didn't go away immediately. But it shifted. It softened just a little. And I felt a wave of sadness that wasn't mine. Or rather, it was mine, but it belonged to a younger part of me. A part that had been waiting decades for someone to notice.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). It explains exactly why parts hold body memories and how to work with them in a way that's actually safe. Not just "feel your feelings" in some vague, wellness-industry way. But a structured, compassionate approach that honors the part's protective function.

The difference between releasing and witnessing

I want to be honest with you about something. There's a lot of messaging out there that says you need to "release" trauma from your body. That you need to shake it out, scream it out, cry it out. And those things can be helpful. But they can also be a form of bypassing. A way of trying to get rid of the part instead of actually being with it.

Your parts don't need to be released. They need to be welcomed. They need to be understood. They need to know that the danger is over, that you're an adult now, that you can handle what they've been carrying. That's not something you can communicate through force. It's something you communicate through presence.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about "pendulation" - the natural rhythm of contraction and expansion that happens when you gently touch a body memory and then come back to a place of safety. You don't stay in the memory. You don't run from it. You approach it, then you return to your window of tolerance. Approach. Return. Approach. Return. Over and over, until the part learns that it's safe to let go.

I did this with a part that held tension in my jaw. I would notice the clenching, acknowledge it, then bring my attention to my feet on the floor. Back to the jaw. Back to the feet. After a few minutes, the jaw softened on its own. Not because I forced it. Because the part felt seen and safe enough to release.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). It's not about fixing the part. It's about accepting that the part exists, that it has a reason for being there, and that it deserves compassion. Even when it's holding something that feels unbearable.

What to do when the body memory feels too big to touch

Sometimes the body memory is so intense that even approaching it feels impossible. Your chest tightens. Your throat closes. Your hands go numb. And a voice inside says, "Don't go there. It's too much."

That voice is a part too. A protector. It's the part that's been keeping you safe from that memory for years. Decades maybe. And it's doing a good job. It's kept you functional. It's kept you alive. So don't push past it. Don't override it. Thank it.

Say to that protector part, "I hear you. I know you're trying to protect me. I'm not going to go anywhere dangerous. I'm just going to sit here with you, right at the edge of this feeling. We don't have to go in."

This is where the work gets real. Because the protector part needs to trust you before it will let you near the younger part that holds the memory. And trust takes time. It takes consistency. It takes showing up day after day, even when nothing seems to change.

I spent weeks just sitting with a protector part that showed up as a wall in my chest. I couldn't get past it. Every time I tried, it got harder. So I stopped trying. I just sat with the wall. I noticed its texture. Its color. Its shape. And eventually, the wall started to talk to me. Not in words. In images. In feelings. In a sense of "I'm keeping something terrible from you." And I said, "I believe you. Thank you for keeping it from me. I'm not ready to see it yet. And that's okay."

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). It frames the whole process in a way that reduces shame and increases curiosity. Because shame is what keeps these parts hidden. Curiosity is what brings them into the light.

Living with the body memories instead of fighting them

Here's where I'm going to be honest in a way that might be uncomfortable. You might never fully "resolve" every body memory. Some of them might always be there, in some form. A slight tension that returns when you're tired. A flutter in your chest when you hear a certain song. A numbness in your hands when you're under stress.

That doesn't mean you failed. It doesn't mean the work didn't work. It means your body learned something deeply, and learning takes time to unlearn. Sometimes, the goal isn't to make the memory disappear. It's to change your relationship to it. To stop being afraid of it. To let it be there without it controlling your life.

I still have parts that hold body memories. I still get that knot in my stomach sometimes. But now, instead of panicking, I say, "Oh, there you are. I remember you. You're the part that holds that thing from when I was seven. You're okay. I'm here. We're safe."

And the knot softens. Not always. But often enough that I trust the process. That I trust the part. That I trust myself.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read that. Did something shift? Did a part of you get curious? Did another part get skeptical? All of that is welcome. All of that is the work.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link)
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts (paid link)
